world-news.title: Trump says Iran deal 'largely negotiated' including reopening Strait of Hormuz
world-news.summary: U.S. President Donald Trump claims he's close to finalizing a major agreement with Iran, a country the U.S. has had hostile relations with for decades. The deal reportedly includes reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway through which about 20% of the world's oil travels—meaning if it's blocked, global energy prices spike and economies suffer. Iran's government is also signaling progress, though they say nuclear weapons (a major concern for Western nations) aren't part of this initial framework. This matters because Iran developing nuclear weapons could destabilize the entire Middle East, and any U.S.-Iran agreement reshapes global politics and energy markets that affect everyone's gas prices.

world-news.title: Rosenberg: Luhansk strike sparks Russian accusations and vow to retaliate
world-news.summary: A missile strike on the town of Luhansk in Russian-occupied eastern Ukraine killed at least 18 people and injured 42 others, escalating the ongoing war between Russia and Ukraine that began in 2022. Russia is accusing Ukraine of the attack and threatening retaliation, while Ukraine claims it struck a Russian military drone unit instead. This conflict matters globally because Ukraine is a major grain supplier (feeding millions worldwide), Russia controls significant oil and gas exports, and the war has already caused widespread inflation and food shortages across Europe and Africa. The constant cycle of attacks and counter-attacks risks spreading the conflict beyond Ukraine's borders.

world-news.title: Rubio meets Modi during India visit with energy high on agenda
world-news.summary: Marco Rubio, the U.S. Secretary of State (the official in charge of international relations), is visiting India to discuss energy deals as part of broader U.S.-India partnership efforts. The U.S. wants to sell energy to India to compensate for shortfalls caused by the Iran war and economic tensions disrupting global energy supplies. This reflects a major geopolitical shift: the U.S. and India are strengthening ties to counter China's growing influence in Asia, and energy deals are central to that strategy. For teenagers, this means the countries you trade with and align with shape everything from job opportunities to prices of goods—and India is becoming increasingly important to America's global influence.

world-news.title: Tens of thousands rally in Serbia for antigovernment demonstrations
world-news.summary: Over tens of thousands of Serbian citizens, many of them students, are protesting their government and demanding early elections. The movement gained momentum after the Novi Sad disaster in November 2024, a tragic event that sparked public anger over government accountability and corruption. Serbia is a Balkan country navigating between alignment with Europe and Russia, making internal political stability important for the entire region's future. This matters because when governments lose public trust, it can lead to instability that affects neighboring countries, refugee crises, and regional security—issues that ripple across Europe and impact global politics.

world-news.title: Putin vows retaliation after accusing Ukraine of hitting student dormitory
world-news.summary: Russian President Vladimir Putin accused Ukraine of striking a student dormitory in a Moscow-occupied region, killing civilians, and vowed to retaliate. Ukraine counters that it actually targeted a military drone facility (the 'Rubicon' unit) in the same area, suggesting Russia misrepresented the target to blame Ukraine for civilian deaths. This escalating cycle of accusations and revenge attacks deepens the Russia-Ukraine war and risks drawing in other countries militarily. For context: when leaders blame each other for civilian casualties, it hardens positions on both sides, makes peace negotiations harder, and increases the danger of the conflict spreading to NATO countries (the Western military alliance) that support Ukraine.

world-news.title: Red Cross volunteers die from suspected Ebola in DR Congo
world-news.summary: Red Cross aid workers in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) have died from suspected Ebola infection, likely contracting the virus before the outbreak was officially identified and proper safety measures were put in place. Ebola is an extremely rare but deadly virus that spreads through direct contact with infected blood or body fluids, with fatality rates sometimes exceeding 50%. This is significant because it shows how quickly dangerous diseases can spread among healthcare workers trying to help, and outbreaks in the DRC are particularly dangerous due to ongoing conflict that disrupts medical response efforts. The death of aid workers also discourages international assistance, making disease control harder and potentially allowing outbreaks to worsen.

world-news.title: Violence and overcrowding hampers Ebola response in DRC
world-news.summary: The Ebola response in the Democratic Republic of Congo is being severely hindered by ongoing violence and overcrowded conditions in hospitals and treatment centers. The DRC is experiencing active armed conflict in several regions, making it dangerous for health workers to reach patients and set up containment measures—basic steps needed to stop disease spread. Overcrowding means infected people are in close contact with uninfected ones, accelerating transmission rates. This creates a perfect storm where a deadly disease spreads faster in exactly the conditions where it's hardest to control, potentially leading to a major regional health catastrophe. International organizations are struggling to respond because instability makes it risky and difficult to deploy medical teams.

world-news.title: Funerals for medics killed in Israeli air strikes in southern Lebanon
world-news.summary: Paramedics and medical workers in southern Lebanon were killed in Israeli air strikes, and funerals are being held for them. Israel and the militant group Hezbollah (based in Lebanon) have escalated military tensions, with Israeli strikes targeting what it claims are militant positions, while Lebanese civilians and aid workers often get caught in the crossfire. This reflects the broader Israeli-Palestinian and Israeli-Lebanese conflict that has destabilized the Middle East for decades. The killing of healthcare workers is particularly serious because it discourages medical personnel from helping wounded people, weakens the healthcare system, and often violates international law protecting medical facilities during conflict.

world-news.title: At least 82 killed in Chinese coal mine explosion
world-news.summary: An explosion at a coal mine in Shanxi province, China killed at least 82 people, making it the country's deadliest mining disaster in 16 years. While mining accidents are often seen as domestic issues, this one matters globally because China is the world's largest coal producer and any major disruption to its mining capacity affects global energy prices and markets. The incident also highlights ongoing workplace safety concerns in China's industrial sector, where regulations are sometimes poorly enforced, raising questions about sustainability of global supply chains dependent on Chinese resources. Major mining disasters can temporarily reduce coal supplies, driving up energy costs worldwide and affecting everything from electricity bills to manufacturing costs.

world-news.title: Secret Service fatally shoots gunman who fired at White House checkpoint
world-news.summary: U.S. Secret Service officers shot and killed a gunman who fired a weapon at a checkpoint near the White House (the residence and workplace of the U.S. President) in Washington, D.C., and a bystander was also wounded in the incident. While this occurred in the U.S., it has international significance because it highlights security threats to America's highest office and reflects broader concerns about gun violence and extremism affecting political stability. The White House is a symbol of American power globally, and any attack on it or its security perimeter draws international attention and can impact diplomatic relations and global markets that react to political uncertainty in the U.S.

finance-markets.title: More War-Driven Inflation Seen in Fed's Favored Gauge
finance-markets.summary: The Federal Reserve—America's central bank that controls interest rates and money supply—uses a specific inflation measurement to track price increases. Right now, that gauge is climbing toward 4%, driven mainly by the Iran war pushing oil prices way up. Inflation means your money buys less stuff (a $5 lunch costs $6). The Fed is worried because if energy prices spike, other prices might follow, making everything more expensive for everyday people. This matters to you because higher inflation can slow down job growth and make saving money less rewarding. The Fed might respond by raising interest rates, which affects student loans, car loans, and future job opportunities.

finance-markets.title: South-east Asian economies struggle to counter energy shock
finance-markets.summary: Southeast Asian countries like Thailand, Vietnam, and Indonesia don't produce much oil domestically, so they depend on imports. The Iran war has disrupted global oil supplies, sending energy prices skyrocketing—a major problem for nations that can't easily afford expensive fuel. These countries are scrambling to protect their economies while fighting inflation (rising prices on everyday goods). When energy costs surge, transportation gets pricier, which makes food and products more expensive for regular people. This creates a domino effect: businesses struggle, workers earn less, and economic growth slows. This situation shows how conflicts halfway around the world can directly impact teenagers' futures through job availability and cost of living, even in countries not directly involved in the war.

finance-markets.title: US oil producers increase output to capture price surge from Iran war
finance-markets.summary: American oil companies are ramping up drilling and production because the Iran conflict has created a shortage, sending oil prices up 40 percent. Higher prices mean more profit for these companies, so they're investing in new wells and equipment. This is actually good news for President Trump's approval ratings since higher oil production creates jobs and more energy independence (less reliance on foreign oil). However, there's a catch: increased supply from US producers could eventually bring global oil prices down, which might reduce profits. This situation shows how global conflicts create business opportunities and risks simultaneously. For teenagers, this means understanding how geopolitical events (conflicts between nations) directly influence job markets and energy costs that affect everything from gas prices to heating bills.

finance-markets.title: Trump says deal with Iran to reopen Hormuz 'largely negotiated'
finance-markets.summary: President Donald Trump announced that a deal with Iran is nearly complete to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway through which about 20 percent of global oil flows. Trump has been meeting with Gulf leaders (Saudi Arabia, UAE, and others) and US allies to finalize terms. The Strait of Hormuz is so important because closing it would cripple global energy supplies and crash the world economy. A successful deal could reduce oil prices, ease inflation, and improve Trump's approval ratings by lowering gas prices. This matters to teenagers because cheaper oil means lower prices at the pump, in stores, and potentially lower interest rates on loans. The announcement signals hope that the Iran conflict could be resolved diplomatically rather than through continued military confrontation.

finance-markets.title: Uber Proposes Delivery Hero Takeover at €10 Billion Valuation
finance-markets.summary: Uber Technologies—the ride-sharing and delivery company—has offered to buy Delivery Hero SE, a German food delivery competitor, for approximately €10 billion (about $11.6 billion). This merger would help Uber compete more effectively against DoorDash, which dominates US food delivery. When large companies buy competitors, it's called a merger or acquisition, and it can affect consumers through changed prices and service quality. Mergers can reduce competition, potentially making services more expensive or lower quality. Regulators (government agencies) carefully scrutinize these deals to ensure they don't harm consumers. For teenagers, this matters because it could affect how you order food, app prices, and delivery fees. It also shows how tech companies constantly reshape industries through massive business deals that reshape how we live.

finance-markets.title: Hungary's New Premier Warns Predecessor Left Budget 'Skeletons'
finance-markets.summary: Peter Magyar, Hungary's newly appointed Prime Minister, is warning that his predecessor left the country's public finances in terrible shape—he's calling these problems 'budget skeletons.' Government budgets are like family budgets: money comes in (taxes) and money goes out (spending). When spending exceeds income for years, you accumulate debt. Magyar is forecasting only 2% economic growth this year because of these inherited problems and can't make firm financial promises to voters. This matters because poor government finances lead to higher taxes, fewer public services (schools, healthcare), and less economic opportunity for young people. When countries have budget crises, they often cut education and social programs that directly affect teenagers. This situation also shows how previous leaders' financial decisions can burden future generations with debt and limited opportunities.

finance-markets.title: Bond slump stirs vigilante fears
finance-markets.summary: Bonds are loans that governments and companies issue to borrow money—investors buy bonds and get paid interest. A 'bond slump' means bond prices are falling and interest rates are rising, which is concerning investment professionals. The fear of 'vigilante' action refers to when bond investors collectively sell bonds due to concerns about government debt or economic stability, forcing interest rates even higher. When government bond rates spike, it makes borrowing more expensive for everyone—students, families, businesses—affecting mortgages, student loans, and business investments. Investment professionals sense 'a serious problem here,' suggesting markets are pricing in economic troubles ahead. For teenagers, this matters because rising interest rates make future loans more expensive and could slow job creation. Bond markets are essentially how governments and big companies borrow money, so when they're stressed, it signals broader economic problems heading your way.

finance-markets.title: US and Iran move closer to extending ceasefire by 60 days, say mediators
finance-markets.summary: International mediators report that the United States and Iran are making progress toward extending their current ceasefire (temporary stop in fighting) by another 60 days. The deal's key term involves gradually reopening the Strait of Hormuz, the critical waterway through which massive amounts of global oil flows. A 60-day extension would give negotiators more time to work toward a permanent peace agreement. This is significant because each extension reduces immediate war risks and helps stabilize global energy markets. When conflicts simmer rather than escalate, oil prices tend to stabilize, reducing inflation pressures worldwide. For teenagers, every month of ceasefire extension means a lower probability of the conflict expanding, which protects global economic stability. Stable energy prices mean more predictable costs for groceries, transportation, and future job opportunities as businesses have clearer economic visibility.

finance-markets.title: The new arms race in computing power
finance-markets.summary: Governments worldwide recognize that military power increasingly depends on data center capability and artificial intelligence (AI)—computational ability to process enormous amounts of information quickly. Nations that are falling behind in AI development are now exploring experimental technologies to catch up. A data center is essentially a massive facility full of specialized computers that power AI systems, cloud computing, and military applications. This 'arms race' means countries are competing to build the most powerful AI capabilities for national defense. This matters to teenagers because whichever nations win this competition will shape global power dynamics for decades. Countries investing heavily in AI and data centers will likely create tech jobs and economic opportunities in those sectors. Additionally, AI advancement will change nearly every career path and job market, making technological literacy increasingly critical for future employment and global competitiveness.

finance-markets.title: World's Most Fervent Day Traders in Korea to Get Risky New Tools
finance-markets.summary: South Korea's stock market—already known for having extremely active day traders (people who buy and sell stocks within single days seeking quick profits)—is about to debut its first single-stock leveraged exchange-traded funds (ETFs). Leveraged means these financial tools use borrowed money to amplify gains and losses. Think of it like this: if you borrow $100 to invest $200 total, you can double your profits—but also double your losses. South Korea's retail investors (regular people, not professionals) are particularly active traders, and this new tool could dramatically increase both their wins and devastating losses. This matters to teenagers because understanding how financial tools work—and their risks—is crucial before investing. The lesson here is that financial innovations that promise bigger gains always come with bigger risks. Young people entering investing should understand that leveraged products are gambling, not stable wealth-building, and can destroy savings quickly.

tech-ai.title: These special phone and app features can help protect you from spyware
tech-ai.summary: Apple, Meta, and Google—the three biggest tech companies that make your phone's operating system—have created special security modes to protect you from spyware, which is malicious software that secretly watches what you do on your device. Spyware is particularly dangerous because it can track your location, read your messages, and access your photos without your knowledge, often through targeted attacks on specific people rather than random hacking. Companies like Apple have introduced "Lockdown Mode" and Google has "Restricted Safe Browsing" to fight this threat. These modes work by limiting certain phone features, blocking suspicious links, and preventing apps from accessing your personal information. Learning how to turn on these protections takes just a few minutes but could save you from serious privacy violations that could affect everything from your personal relationships to your safety.

tech-ai.title: Google's AI search is so broken it can 'disregard' what you're looking for
tech-ai.summary: Google recently rolled out "AI Overviews," a feature that uses artificial intelligence to summarize search results directly on your search page so you don't have to click through multiple websites. However, the AI system has been making embarrassing mistakes—like when you search for the word "disregard," the AI sometimes responds with generic chat-bot phrases like "Got it. If you need anything else, just let me know!" instead of actually answering your search question. This problem shows a fundamental flaw in how Google's AI understands what users actually want, because it's sometimes ignoring the search term entirely and behaving like a personal assistant instead. These kinds of errors matter because millions of teenagers and adults rely on Google to find accurate information for homework, research, and daily questions, and broken AI could spread misinformation or make it harder to find what you need.

tech-ai.title: Google's new anything-to-anything AI model is wild
tech-ai.summary: Google has created a new AI model called Gemini that can take almost any type of input—text, images, video, audio—and transform it into almost any other type of output, which is why tech experts are calling it "anything-to-anything." This is more powerful than previous AI systems, which typically worked with just one type of information at a time. The AI can do things like turn a stuffed animal into a video of that animal on vacation, create realistic deepfakes (fake videos that look real), or generate complex creative content that combines multiple forms of media. While this technology has exciting creative uses, it also raises serious concerns about misinformation and misuse, because bad actors could use it to create convincing fake videos of politicians, celebrities, or people you know saying things they never said, which could spread false information quickly.

tech-ai.title: SolarSquare in talks to raise up to $60M as India's rooftop solar market draws major VC interest
tech-ai.summary: SolarSquare is an Indian company that helps homes and businesses install solar panels on their roofs as a way to generate clean, renewable energy from the sun instead of relying on power grids powered by fossil fuels. The company is in discussions to raise $60 million (a massive amount of money) from venture capitalists—wealthy investors who fund promising startup companies—and could be valued at up to $500 million, meaning investors believe it could become a half-billion-dollar company. This financing is happening because India has massive potential for rooftop solar since it gets abundant sunlight year-round, and more Indians are looking for ways to reduce their electricity costs and carbon footprint (the environmental impact of their energy use). The success of SolarSquare shows a global trend where investors are betting big on clean energy solutions, and for teenagers, it matters because the renewable energy industry will likely create millions of jobs and influence what energy sources power your future homes and schools.

tech-ai.title: Ferrari is using IBM's AI to create F1 superfans
tech-ai.summary: Ferrari, the legendary Italian luxury car company famous for its red sports cars, has partnered with IBM (one of the world's oldest and largest technology companies) to use artificial intelligence to completely change how Formula 1 racing fans experience the sport. Instead of just watching races passively, Ferrari's AI system can personalize the experience for each fan by predicting what they'll want to see, recommending content about their favorite drivers, and creating customized highlights from races. The AI learns from how fans interact with Ferrari content—what videos they watch, what they comment on, what they ignore—and then uses that data to serve them exactly what they want, kind of like how Netflix recommends shows or Spotify suggests songs. This matters because it shows how AI is being used to deepen fan engagement in sports, and it could change how you experience entertainment in the future, whether that's sports, music, or gaming, by making everything feel personalized just for you.

tech-ai.title: Peec, one of Berlin's rising startups, more than doubled annualized revenue in months to $10M
tech-ai.summary: Peec is a Berlin-based startup (a new company in Germany's capital) that helps brands understand and track how they appear in AI search results—basically, when you use ChatGPT or other AI chatbots to search for information, Peec tells companies how often their products or brand get mentioned. The company has grown incredibly fast, doubling its annual revenue (the total money it makes per year) in just a few months to reach $10 million, which is remarkable growth for such a young company. This explosive growth reveals an important trend: as more people use AI chatbots instead of traditional Google searches to find information, companies are panicking about their online visibility and will pay money to understand how AI systems represent their brands. For teenagers, this matters because it shows how the shift from traditional search to AI-powered search is creating entirely new business opportunities, and it signals that the way we find information online is fundamentally changing in real-time.

tech-ai.title: SpaceX's Starship V3—still a work in progress—mostly successful on first flight
tech-ai.summary: SpaceX, the rocket company founded by Elon Musk, has tested its latest version of Starship (an enormous reusable spacecraft designed to carry humans and cargo to space) and the flight was mostly successful, though the vehicle still needs significant improvements before it can fly humans safely to space or establish a moon base. Starship is one of the most ambitious space projects ever attempted—the goal is to create a fully reusable spacecraft that can launch, land, and launch again, similar to how airplanes work, which would dramatically reduce the cost of space travel. The "mostly successful" test means some things worked as planned but other systems malfunctioned or need refinement before the next flight attempt. For teenagers interested in space, science, or technology careers, this matters because SpaceX is on the cutting edge of making space more accessible and affordable, and successful spaceflight technology could eventually make space travel available to regular people, not just astronauts.

tech-ai.title: Elon Musk has given up on solar power (on Earth)
tech-ai.summary: Elon Musk, the billionaire founder of Tesla (an electric car company) and SpaceX, has famously talked about creating a "solar-electric economy" where homes and cars run on clean solar power instead of fossil fuels—but his own companies are now moving away from that vision. His AI company xAI has instead invested heavily in natural gas (a fossil fuel that produces carbon emissions when burned), while SpaceX is building massive data centers in space that would require enormous amounts of electricity from any available source. This shift is surprising and disappointing to many environmental advocates because Musk built his reputation on promoting clean energy solutions and sustainable technology. For teenagers, this matters because it shows that even influential tech billionaires sometimes abandon their public promises, and it highlights the gap between what powerful people say they believe and what they actually invest money in, which is an important lesson about thinking critically about corporate environmental claims.

tech-ai.title: Nuclear startup Deep Fission says it's going public, again, and I have questions
tech-ai.summary: Deep Fission is a nuclear energy startup—a company building new technology to generate electricity from nuclear power—that is planning an initial public offering (IPO), which means the company wants to sell shares (pieces of ownership) to regular investors on the stock market to raise $157 million. Going public is normally a major achievement for startups, but journalists and investors are skeptical about Deep Fission's plans because the company's story doesn't quite add up, and there are concerns about whether it can actually deliver on its promises to build safe, cost-effective nuclear reactors. This skepticism is important because nuclear energy is a complex, heavily regulated industry with high barriers to entry, and startups making big promises about nuclear technology should be carefully scrutinized before people invest their money. For teenagers interested in energy and climate solutions, this highlights how important it is to think critically about tech companies' claims and to understand that not every startup with a good pitch will succeed.

tech-ai.title: Record Club is trying to be Letterboxd for music nerds
tech-ai.summary: Record Club is a new social media app designed specifically for music lovers, modeled after Letterboxd, which is a hugely popular platform where people catalog the movies they watch, rate them, write reviews, and connect with other film fans who have similar tastes. While websites like Rate Your Music already exist for people who want to track their music listening, Record Club aims to create a cleaner, more modern, and user-friendly experience that makes it easier to log songs you've listened to, discover new music through recommendations, and join communities of people who love the same artists and genres. The app represents a growing trend of "niche social networks"—platforms built specifically for particular interests rather than being general-purpose like Facebook or Instagram, which allows for deeper connections between people who share passionate interests. For teenagers, this matters because it's a tool that lets you find your music community online, discover new artists, and express your musical identity in a dedicated space designed just for that purpose.

science-space.title: Scientists just found a faster, cleaner way to extract lithium for EV batteries
science-space.summary: Researchers at Columbia Engineering, a prestigious university lab in New York, have developed a revolutionary method to extract lithium — a critical mineral needed for electric vehicle (EV) batteries — more efficiently and sustainably than ever before. Lithium extraction has traditionally relied on massive evaporation ponds that take years to produce usable material and consume enormous amounts of water in already dry regions. This new technique uses a temperature-sensitive solvent to pull lithium directly from underground salty brines, cutting extraction time dramatically while preserving precious water resources. What makes this breakthrough even more important is that it works on low-quality lithium sources that current technology can't use, potentially opening up new mining sites worldwide. As electric vehicles become more common and we need more batteries for phones, computers, and renewable energy storage, this innovation could make clean energy technology far more sustainable and affordable for everyone.

science-space.title: Watch China launch Shenzhou 23 astronauts to its Tiangong space station on May 24
science-space.summary: China is preparing to launch its Shenzhou 23 mission on May 24, 2026, sending three astronauts to the Tiangong space station — China's permanently crewed orbital laboratory orbiting Earth at about 250 miles up. This mission is historically significant because it includes Hong Kong's first astronaut, marking a major achievement for the region and showing China's growing leadership in human spaceflight exploration. The Shenzhou spacecraft (the name means 'Divine Vessel' in Chinese) will relieve the current crew on Tiangong and potentially begin China's first year-long continuous human presence in space, matching achievements of other spacefaring nations like the United States and Russia. The Tiangong space station, which has been continuously crewed since 2021, conducts scientific experiments in microgravity — an environment where gravity is nearly zero — that can't be done on Earth. This represents a major milestone in China's space program and demonstrates how space exploration is becoming increasingly international, with different nations reaching new heights in human spaceflight.

science-space.title: China reveals 3-person Shenzhou 23 crew, including Hong Kong's 1st astronaut
science-space.summary: China has officially announced the three astronauts selected for the Shenzhou 23 mission, a major crewed spaceflight launching on May 24, 2026, to the Tiangong space station orbiting Earth. Among the crew is a historic figure: Hong Kong's first-ever astronaut, representing a significant milestone for the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, which is part of China. These carefully selected astronauts have undergone years of rigorous training at China's astronaut training center, learning everything from spacecraft operations to emergency procedures and scientific research methods in microgravity — the weightless environment of space. Their mission will involve conducting experiments, maintaining the Tiangong station, and potentially preparing for longer-term human spaceflight missions, including future lunar exploration goals China has announced. This achievement highlights how space exploration has become a source of national pride and international cooperation, bringing together people from different regions and nations in humanity's quest to explore beyond Earth and advance scientific knowledge.

science-space.title: SpaceX Starship Flight 12: Don't miss these stunning photos from the launch of the most powerful Starship yet
science-space.summary: SpaceX, the private space company founded by Elon Musk, has successfully launched its 12th test flight of the Starship — the world's most powerful rocket ever built, designed to eventually land astronauts on the Moon and Mars. This particular flight, Starship V3, represents the most advanced version of the vehicle to date, incorporating improvements from previous flights and pushing the boundaries of what reusable rocket technology can achieve. The Starship is built specifically for deep-space missions, as opposed to regular satellites or trips to orbit — it's designed to carry huge payloads (cargo) and eventually humans on multi-month journeys to other worlds. Each test flight provides engineers with crucial data about how the rocket performs during takeoff, in space, and during re-entry, helping SpaceX refine the technology toward making Mars colonization possible. The stunning launch photography from Flight 12 captures the sheer power and scale of this engineering achievement: a 400-foot-tall vehicle producing 33 mega-engines firing simultaneously, generating more thrust than any rocket in history and representing a major step forward in humanity's capability to explore deep space.

science-space.title: Wegovy linked to rare 'eye stroke' that can cause sudden blindness
science-space.summary: Researchers analyzing millions of side-effect reports submitted to the FDA (Food and Drug Administration, the U.S. agency that approves medicines) have raised new safety concerns about Wegovy, an extremely popular weight-loss drug that works by mimicking a hormone called GLP-1 that controls hunger and blood sugar. The study found that Wegovy carries a significantly higher risk of causing a rare condition called an 'eye stroke' — technically known as retinal artery occlusion — which occurs when blood flow to the eye's retina (the light-sensing tissue at the back of your eye) becomes suddenly blocked, potentially causing immediate vision loss or blindness. Interestingly, both Wegovy and Ozempic (another popular medication) contain the same active ingredient called semaglutide, yet Wegovy users reported eye stroke symptoms nearly five times more frequently, raising questions about why this difference exists and whether other factors might be involved. This finding doesn't mean Wegovy is unsafe for everyone — millions use it successfully — but it highlights why pharmaceutical companies and doctors must carefully monitor medications' effects once they're widely used, not just during initial testing. For teenagers considering weight-loss medications in the future, this research demonstrates the importance of discussing all potential side effects with doctors and weighing risks against benefits.

science-space.title: Scientists warn that current vitamin B12 guidelines may be putting your brain at risk
science-space.summary: Researchers at UC San Francisco, one of America's leading medical research institutions, have discovered that following the government's current recommended daily allowance for vitamin B12 might not actually protect your brain as you age, especially once you reach your 60s and beyond. Their study found that older adults with B12 levels considered 'normal' according to official health guidelines still showed signs of cognitive decline — including slower thinking speed, delayed visual processing (how quickly your eyes and brain work together), and damage to white matter, which are the brain's communication highways connecting different regions so they can work together. Vitamin B12, found naturally in animal products like meat, fish, eggs, and dairy, is essential for brain function and nerve health, but as people age, their ability to absorb B12 from food decreases significantly. This research suggests that the current official guidelines may have been set too low and that older adults might benefit from higher B12 levels to protect their brain health as they age. For teenagers, this finding highlights why nutritional science evolves over time and why we sometimes need to update health recommendations as researchers learn more, plus it emphasizes the long-term importance of good nutrition for brain health throughout your life.

science-space.title: Scientists discover simple way to relieve arthritis pain without pills or surgery
science-space.summary: Researchers conducting a year-long clinical trial — a rigorous scientific study involving real people following a treatment over an extended period — have discovered a surprisingly simple solution for knee osteoarthritis pain: slightly changing the angle of your foot while walking can reduce pain just as effectively as medications commonly prescribed for arthritis. Osteoarthritis is a condition where the protective cartilage (the smooth tissue coating bone ends in joints) gradually wears away, causing pain, stiffness, and reduced mobility, affecting millions of people worldwide, especially as they age. The study found that this minor gait modification (how you walk) not only decreased pain but actually slowed the rate of cartilage damage happening inside the joint, meaning it addresses the underlying problem rather than just masking symptoms like pain medication does. This breakthrough is significant because it offers people with arthritis a non-pharmaceutical option — meaning no pills with side effects or risks — and no invasive surgery, simply changing biomechanics (how your body moves) to reduce joint stress. For teenagers, this research demonstrates how sometimes the simplest solutions can be the most effective, and how understanding body mechanics can help prevent injuries and manage future health issues throughout your life.

science-space.title: Scientists uncover cancer-causing chemicals hidden in everyday foods
science-space.summary: Food scientists have identified potentially cancer-causing chemicals called PAHs (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons) that can form in many everyday foods, particularly when they're cooked using high-heat methods like grilling, roasting, smoking, and frying — the same cooking techniques that make food taste delicious and appealing. PAHs are organic compounds created when food is exposed to extreme heat or flames, and they've been classified as potentially carcinogenic (cancer-causing) by health organizations, meaning repeated exposure over years might increase cancer risk. These chemicals don't just form during cooking; they can also enter foods through environmental contamination from air pollution and industrial sources, adding to the overall exposure people experience in their daily lives. The concern is particularly relevant because grilled burgers, roasted meats, smoked fish, and fried foods are staples in many people's diets, and the long-term health effects of consuming low levels of PAHs remain an active area of research. While this doesn't mean you need to eliminate grilled or roasted foods entirely, the research suggests that varying your cooking methods, not overcooking foods until they're charred, and balancing your diet with plenty of fresh fruits and vegetables can help minimize potential risks while still enjoying a wide variety of foods.

science-space.title: Scientists warn popular vitamin D supplement may have a 'previously unknown' downside
science-space.summary: A surprising new study has revealed that vitamin D2 supplements — one of the two main forms of supplemental vitamin D that millions of people take for bone health and immune function — may actually interfere with the body's ability to maintain adequate levels of vitamin D3, which is the more effective form of vitamin D your body naturally produces. Researchers discovered that vitamin D3 not only boosts overall vitamin D status more efficiently than D2, but it may also play a unique and important role in helping your immune system fight off viruses and bacteria that cause infections and illness. Vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) is synthetic, meaning it's manufactured in laboratories, while vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) can be made by your body when skin is exposed to sunlight or comes from animal-based foods, and the two forms work differently in your body. This discovery is prompting scientists and medical organizations worldwide to reconsider their supplement recommendations, potentially shifting toward D3 as the preferred choice for people who need supplementation rather than relying on sunlight exposure. For teenagers, this research demonstrates how nutritional science continues evolving as researchers uncover new information, and why it's important to stay informed about supplements and discuss them with doctors or nutritionists rather than assuming all vitamins work the same way.

science-space.title: Einstein's 'wormhole' may actually reveal a hidden mirror of time
science-space.summary: Theoretical physicists have proposed a groundbreaking new interpretation of wormholes — the hypothetical tunnels through spacetime that Einstein theorized might connect distant regions of the universe — suggesting they might not actually be cosmic shortcuts at all, but rather bridges connecting two different directions of time itself. Wormholes (also called Einstein-Rosen bridges) have long fascinated scientists as a theoretical possibility permitted by Einstein's equations of general relativity (the theory explaining gravity), though none have ever been observed or confirmed to exist in the universe. This new research suggests that wormholes could actually be a window into quantum physics (the science of incredibly tiny subatomic particles), potentially connecting parallel versions of time flowing in opposite directions deep within black holes — regions of space with gravity so intense that nothing, not even light, can escape. The implications are staggering: this theory might solve the famous 'black hole information paradox,' a decades-old puzzle about what happens to information when it falls into a black hole, and it hints at something even stranger — that our universe might have existed before the Big Bang (the explosive beginning of space and time). While this remains highly speculative theoretical physics that may never be proven, it showcases how scientists continually reimagine the universe's fundamental nature and how reality itself might work in ways far stranger than we imagine.

energy-climate.title: EU understimating rooftop PV power generation

The group attributes the gap to incomplete rooftop PV registration, data transfer delays, and the difficulty of measuring self-consumed solar electricity.

energy-climate.title: Mandatory utility fees distort price signals for rooftop solar in the US
energy-climate.summary: Regulators in 27 U.S. states are shifting residential electricity pricing toward higher fixed monthly charges and lower usage-based rates, weakening the economics of rooftop solar and home battery systems. This redesign reduces incentives for distributed energy, compresses savings from peak-rate arbitrage, and reinforces reliance on costly centralized grid expansion, potentially raising long-term electricity costs for consumers.
